chunk_summary: Objective monitoring of pediatric IBD includes PUCAI for severity grading and fecal calprotectin (FC) for mucosal inflammation assessment, aiding in treatment decisions and preventing complications like anemia and growth delays.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of PUCAI in grading ulcerative colitis severity and monitoring treatment response.
Learn pediatric-specific thresholds for fecal calprotectin in ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease.
Identify clinical and laboratory markers for objective risk assessment in pediatric IBD.
Recognize the importance of timely escalation in treatment to prevent malnutrition and growth impairment.
key_facts: PUCAI ≥65 indicates acute severe colitis requiring admission.
Clinically meaningful PUCAI improvement is ≥20 points or remission entry.
Fecal calprotectin >250 mg/g indicates active mucosal inflammation in pediatric ulcerative colitis.
Low fecal calprotectin (<250 mg/g) after induction therapy predicts favorable outcomes in pediatric Crohn's disease.
Healthy fecal calprotectin values in children >4 years may reach ~100 mg/g, with adult-like thresholds <50 mg/g.
Objective risk markers include blood in stool, weight loss, anemia, low albumin, and falling height velocity.
reference_quote: Fecal calprotectin (FC) >250 mg/g is used as evidence of active mucosal inflammation even when symptoms improve. (Source: ESPGHAN position paper)
document_key_topics: PUCAI scoring for ulcerative colitis severity
Fecal calprotectin thresholds in pediatric IBD
Objective risk markers for IBD progression
Impact of IBD on pediatric growth and nutrition
